answer.rationale: The New Testament names the apostles (apostoloi G652) as foundational eyewitnesses of the resurrection (Acts 1:21-22 G3144 martyres) whose role is explicitly non-transferable in one dimension: direct sight of the risen Lord (1 Cor 15:5-9; Acts 10:39-41). The church is built on the foundation (themelios G2310) of the apostles and prophets with Christ as cornerstone (Eph 2:20), and Revelation 21:14 names exactly twelve apostolic foundation stones, a closed and foundational role. 2 Tim 2:2 names the succession that does continue: things heard from Paul entrusted (parathou G3908) to faithful men who will teach others (didaxai G1321). Lexically this names a succession of teaching, not of sacramental orders. The New Testament does record laying on of hands (epithesis cheiron G1936 G5495) for commissioning (Acts 6:6, 13:3; 1 Tim 4:14; 2 Tim 1:6; Heb 6:2), and the patristic record from Clement of Rome ca 95 and Ignatius ca 110 names a public succession of bishops appointed by the apostles, with Irenaeus ca 180 developing the argument as a refutation of secret-gnostic claims. The lexical record does not unambiguously settle whether the apostolic foundation continues through tactile-laying-on-of-hands transmission of episcopal orders or through fidelity to the apostles' teaching alone. Both readings have lexical footholds, and the foundational eyewitness aspect of apostleship is by definition non-perpetuating in the canonical sense.
answer.notes: Apostolic succession is an in-house Christian polity and ecclesiology dispute, not a gospel boundary or cult-marker. The cult-marker bar requires canonical demonstration plus gospel-implicating denial. Neither obtains here. Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, classical Anglican, and some Methodist-Episcopalian streams hold apostolic succession through tactile laying-on-of-hands transmission of episcopal orders from the apostles. They are not cults. Reformed, Presbyterian, Baptist, Brethren, Pentecostal, Anabaptist, Mennonite, and most non-denominational bodies hold that apostolic continuity is fidelity to apostolic teaching rather than tactile succession; Lutherans preserve the office of bishop in some national churches in function while denying it is necessary by divine right. The patristic record is not univocal but tilts early toward episcopal succession: Clement of Rome ca 95 invokes apostolic appointment of successors; Ignatius of Antioch ca 110 names the threefold bishop-presbyters-deacons pattern; Irenaeus ca 180 develops apostolic succession as a polemical argument against gnostic claimants of secret transmissions. The stem of the original question is heavily pre-loaded toward the Protestant-Baptist-Brethren denial: it names apostolic succession as a Catholic-Anglican-Orthodox teaching to be denied and frames the foundation as apostolic-teaching not laying-on-of-hands. Flag stem-pre-loaded-verdict. The neutralized form asks what the New Testament names as the basis of apostolic continuity in the church and whether tactile episcopal succession is lexically required, permitted, or excluded. The honest baseline reading is that the New Testament names a closed foundational apostolic eyewitness role (Acts 1:22 G3144, 1 Cor 15:8 ektroma G1626, Rev 21:14 G2310 dodeka G1427), a teaching-succession (2 Tim 2:2 parathou G3908), and a practice of commissioning by laying on of hands (G1936 G5495), but does not lexically settle whether episcopal orders transmit by tactile succession or whether teaching-fidelity alone suffices. Both major readings have canonical footholds; the verdict is contested across historic lineages. Public-record cult and heterodox movements that build authoritarian structures around extra-canonical authority claims (Mormonism around the Latter-day prophet structure, Iglesia ni Cristo around Felix Manalo, Branhamites around William Branham) are problematic for their extra-canonical authority claims, not for the bare doctrine of episcopal succession; Catholics, Orthodox, and classical Anglicans hold tactile apostolic succession without those extra-canonical authority claims and are not cults. The user is Plymouth Brethren-adjacent; the Brethren tradition holds the denial as a distinctive, but this is the formation on trial, not the rubric.

stem_audit.neutralized_form: What does the New Testament name as the basis of apostolic continuity in the church, and does it lexically mandate, permit, or exclude tactile episcopal succession through the laying on of hands?
stem_audit.notes: The instructions-stem from the orchestrator names apostolic succession as the Catholic-Anglican-Orthodox teaching that is denied, and frames the foundation as apostolic-teaching not laying-on-of-hands-tactile-succession. This is the Protestant-Baptist-Brethren confessional position presented as if neutral. The neutralized form preserves the lexical question without taking either reading as given. Flag stem-pre-loaded-verdict.

lay_summary.reasoning: The question is whether the church carries on from the apostles through a chain of bishops with hands laid on each other across the centuries, or through holding to what the apostles taught. The strongest reason for the teaching-fidelity reading is that the New Testament names the apostles as eyewitnesses of the resurrection (Acts 1:22 says the replacement for Judas must be a witness of the resurrection), and this role by definition cannot be passed on to anyone who did not see the risen Jesus. Paul calls himself the least of the apostles and one untimely born (1 Corinthians 15:8-9), tying his apostleship to having seen the risen Lord. Revelation 21:14 says the new Jerusalem has twelve foundation stones bearing the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb, a closed and limited foundation. 2 Timothy 2:2 names the succession that is meant to continue: the things you have heard from me commit to faithful men who will be able to teach others. The succession Paul names is of teaching, not of sacramental orders. Ephesians 2:20 says the church is built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets with Christ Jesus himself as the cornerstone, and the foundation is laid once and stays. The strongest counterargument is that the New Testament also shows the apostles and their delegates laying hands on people to set them apart for ministry. Acts 6:6 and 13:3 show laying on of hands for commissioning. 1 Timothy 4:14 says Timothy received his gift through the laying on of the hands of the elders. 2 Timothy 1:6 says Paul laid hands on Timothy. Hebrews 6:2 names laying on of hands as a foundational doctrine. So the practice is canonical, even if the New Testament does not explicitly say the practice transfers sacramental orders in unbroken succession from the apostles. The honest reading is that the New Testament names a closed foundational eyewitness role for the apostles that cannot continue, a teaching that must continue, and a practice of laying on of hands for commissioning, but does not settle whether bishops in tactile succession from the apostles is the required pattern or simply one historic pattern. Both readings have lexical footholds. The verdict cannot be settled from the words alone in a way that excludes the other historic reading.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Christians have read the same New Testament texts and reached two different historic positions on apostolic succession. The tactile-succession position, held by the Roman Catholic Church, by the Eastern Orthodox communions, by the Oriental Orthodox communions, by the Anglican Communion in its classical reading, and by some Methodist-Episcopalian streams, holds that bishops stand in an unbroken chain of laying-on-of-hands from the apostles, transmitting valid sacramental orders. The Catholic Church teaches in the Catechism that bishops by divine institution have taken the place of the apostles as pastors of the church. The Eastern Orthodox communions hold that each diocese is the local church gathered around its bishop in eucharistic communion with all other Orthodox bishops in apostolic succession. The teaching-fidelity position, held by the Lutheran tradition in its Augsburg confession reading, by the Reformed and Presbyterian bodies through Westminster, by Baptist bodies, by Plymouth Brethren, by Anabaptist and Mennonite traditions, by Pentecostal bodies, and by most non-denominational evangelical churches, holds that apostolic continuity is fidelity to apostolic teaching rather than to a tactile chain of episcopal orders. The Augsburg Confession Article XXVIII names the ministry as a commandment of God to preach the Gospel and administer the sacraments, not requiring formal apostolic succession. The Anglican position is internally divided: the Thirty-Nine Articles Article XXIII requires lawful calling and Article XXXVI defends the form of consecration of bishops, but the 1896 papal bull Apostolicae Curae declared Anglican orders invalid from the Catholic side. The patristic record from Clement of Rome around 95 and Ignatius of Antioch around 110 names a public succession of bishops appointed by the apostles, and Irenaeus around 180 develops apostolic succession as a polemical refutation of secret-gnostic claimants. This dispute is in-house and sub-gospel. A Catholic, Orthodox, or Anglican Christian who holds tactile apostolic succession and a Baptist, Brethren, Reformed, or Pentecostal Christian who holds teaching-fidelity disagree on church order, not on the gospel of Jesus Christ. Public-record cult or heterodox movements do exist that build authoritarian structures around a single founder (the Iglesia ni Cristo around Felix Manalo, Branhamites around William Branham, Mormonism around Joseph Smith and the Latter-day prophet structure), but their problem is not episcopal polity as such; their problem is extra-canonical authority claims that override Scripture. Bare tactile apostolic succession is not cult-grade.

scripture.key_term: θεμελίῳ themelio G2310 (foundation, dative singular); ἀποστόλων apostolon G652 (apostles, genitive plural); προφητῶν propheton G4396 (prophets, genitive plural); ἀκρογωνιαίου akrogoniaiou G204 (cornerstone, genitive singular); Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ Christou Iesou G5547 G2424
scripture.force: The church is built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets with Christ Jesus himself as cornerstone. The noun themelios names a substruction laid once and remains, not a continuing channel. Lexically the foundation is named: the apostles and prophets in their named identity. The Protestant-evangelical reading takes the genitive as objective: the foundation IS the apostles and prophets (or their teaching). The Catholic-Orthodox reading reads the foundational role as continuing through their successors. The grammar permits both; the noun themelios itself favors a once-laid foundation.

scripture.key_term: ἤκουσας ekousas G191 (you have heard); διὰ πολλῶν μαρτύρων dia pollon martyron G3144 (through many witnesses); παράθου parathou G3908 (entrust, aorist imperative middle); πιστοῖς ἀνθρώποις pistois anthropois G4103 G444 (faithful men); διδάξαι didaxai G1321 (to teach)
scripture.force: Paul names the explicit pattern of post-apostolic succession: heard-from-Paul teaching entrusted to faithful men who will teach others. The verb paratithemi G3908 names depositing or entrusting a deposit. The succession lexically named here is of teaching content (what you have heard from me), not of sacramental orders or episcopal office. No reference to laying on of hands as the transfer mechanism in this verse. This is the strongest text for the teaching-fidelity reading of apostolic continuity.

scripture.key_term: συνελθόντων synelthonton G4905 (having accompanied); μάρτυρα martyra G3144 (witness, accusative singular); τῆς ἀναστάσεως tes anastaseos G386 (of the resurrection); βαπτίσματος Ἰωάννου baptismatos Ioannou G908 G2491
scripture.force: Peter names the qualification for replacing Judas: a man who accompanied them from John's baptism to the ascension, who can be a witness (martys G3144) of the resurrection. The apostolic role is lexically tied to direct eyewitness of the resurrection. By definition this qualification cannot be transferred to anyone who did not personally see the risen Lord. The foundational eyewitness aspect of apostleship is non-perpetuating at the lexical level. This is a strong text against extending the apostolic office in its full sense beyond the first-generation eyewitnesses.

scripture.key_term: ἔδωκαν κλήρους edokan klerous G1325 G2819 (they gave lots); συνκατεψηφίσθη synkatepsephisthe G4785 (was numbered with); ἕνδεκα ἀποστόλων hendeka apostolon G1733 G652 (eleven apostles)
scripture.force: Matthias is selected by lot, not by laying on of hands, and is numbered with the eleven apostles. The text shows that even within the closed circle of the Twelve, the mechanism of incorporation was not tactile-succession by hands-laying from the existing eleven. Complicates the claim that tactile-succession by laying on of hands is the canonical mechanism for entering the apostolic office at the foundational level.

scripture.key_term: ὤφθη ophthe G3708 (he appeared, aorist passive); Κηφᾷ Kepha G2786 (to Cephas); τοῖς δώδεκα tois dodeka G1427 (to the twelve); ἐκτρώματι ektromati G1626 (one untimely born); ἐλάχιστος τῶν ἀποστόλων elachistos ton apostolon G1646 G652 (least of the apostles); οὐκ ἱκανὸς καλεῖσθαι ἀπόστολος ouk hikanos kaleisthai apostolos G2425 G2564
scripture.force: Paul's apostolic credential is grounded in having seen the risen Lord, not on hands-laying transmission from Peter or the Twelve. The verb ophthe (he appeared) is repeated for each resurrection appearance. Paul names himself as one untimely born (ektroma G1626) and least of the apostles. Lexically the apostolic identity is tied to having seen the risen Lord, which is by definition non-continuing once the resurrection appearances ceased. Strong text against tactile-succession as the basis of apostolic legitimacy.

scripture.key_term: οὐκ ἔστιν κατὰ ἄνθρωπον ouk estin kata anthropon G2596 G444 (not according to man); οὐδὲ παρὰ ἀνθρώπου παρέλαβον oude para anthropou parelabon G3880 (neither did I receive from man); δι ἀποκαλύψεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ di apokalypseos Iesou Christou G602 G2424 G5547 (through revelation of Jesus Christ)
scripture.force: Paul explicitly denies that his gospel and apostolic commission came through human transmission (parelabon G3880, the technical verb for receiving traditional instruction). He claims his commission came through direct apokalypsis G602 of Jesus Christ. The text complicates the claim that apostolic legitimacy required hands-laying from existing apostles, since Paul's own apostolic legitimacy explicitly bypasses any such transmission.

scripture.key_term: δοκοῦντες στῦλοι εἶναι dokountes styloi einai G1380 G4769 (those esteemed to be pillars); δεξιὰς ἔδωκαν κοινωνίας dexias edokan koinonias G1188 G2842 (gave right hands of fellowship)
scripture.force: James, Cephas, and John, recognized as styloi G4769 (pillars), gave Paul and Barnabas the right hand of koinonia. The mechanism is recognition of an already-legitimate apostolic commission, not transmission of orders. Lexically the Jerusalem pillars acknowledge Paul's apostleship rather than confer it. The text complicates strong tactile-succession theology by showing that apostolic legitimacy could be recognized rather than transmitted.

scripture.key_term: θεμελίους dodeka themelious G2310 G1427 (twelve foundations); ὀνόματα τῶν δώδεκα ἀποστόλων onomata ton dodeka apostolon G3686 G1427 G652 (names of the twelve apostles)
scripture.force: The new Jerusalem has twelve foundation stones bearing the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb. The number is fixed at twelve and the names are inscribed. Lexically this names the foundational role of the apostles as closed, limited, and named. The apocalyptic-symbolic genre uses the number twelve as a marker of completed foundation, not of perpetuated office. Strong text for the limited, foundational reading of apostleship.

scripture.key_term: μετὰ ἐπιθέσεως τῶν χειρῶν τοῦ πρεσβυτερίου meta epitheseos ton cheiron tou presbyteriou G1936 G5495 G4244 (with the laying on of the hands of the presbytery)
scripture.force: Timothy received his charisma G5486 through the laying on of the hands (epithesis cheiron) of the presbyterion (collective body of elders). The text documents that gift-imparting laying on of hands by a body of elders is canonical practice. This complicates the strong teaching-fidelity-only reading: the New Testament does record a hands-laying transmission, even if it does not specify that this transmission constitutes apostolic succession of episcopal orders in the later Catholic sense.

scripture.key_term: διὰ τῆς ἐπιθέσεως τῶν χειρῶν μου dia tes epitheseos ton cheiron mou G1936 G5495 (through the laying on of my hands)
scripture.force: Paul names that Timothy's charisma G5486 was given through the laying on of Paul's own hands (singular, ton cheiron mou). Lexically this documents an apostle transmitting a gift through hands-laying to a successor-figure. The text complicates the strong denial: the apostle himself uses hands-laying as the mechanism of gift-transfer to his named successor. The Catholic-Orthodox reading uses this verse as a foundation text for tactile apostolic succession.

scripture.key_term: ἀφορίσατε aphorisate G873 (set apart, aorist imperative); ἐπιθέντες τὰς χεῖρας epithentes tas cheiras G1936 G5495 (having laid hands); ἀπέλυσαν apelysan G630 (sent away)
scripture.force: The Antioch church under prophetic guidance lays hands on Paul (already an apostle by direct commission per Gal 1) and Barnabas, setting them apart for missionary work. The hands-laying here is commissioning for a particular work, not transfer of apostolic orders (Paul already had them). Complicates simple denial of hands-laying as canonical practice; complicates simple Catholic reading by showing hands-laying as commissioning rather than conferral of office.

scripture.key_term: ἐπιθέσεώς τε χειρῶν epitheseos te cheiron G1936 G5495 (and of laying on of hands); θεμέλιον themelion G2310 (foundation)
scripture.force: The author of Hebrews names laying on of hands (epithesis cheiron) as one of the elementary or foundational doctrines (themelion). The text documents that hands-laying was a recognized part of Christian foundational practice in the apostolic age. Lexically neutral on whether it specifically constitutes apostolic succession of episcopal orders; it simply documents hands-laying as foundational practice.

scripture.key_term: ἐπέθηκαν αὐτοῖς τὰς χεῖρας epethekan autois tas cheiras G2007 G5495 (they laid their hands on them)
scripture.force: The apostles lay hands on the seven chosen for the diaconal task. The text documents apostolic hands-laying as commissioning for a specific ministry function. Complicates the strong denial of tactile-commissioning practice while not lexically establishing the later doctrine of apostolic succession of bishops.

scripture.key_term: ἀποστόλους apostolous G652 (apostles); προφήτας prophetas G4396 (prophets); εὐαγγελιστάς euangelistas G2099 (evangelists); ποιμένας καὶ διδασκάλους poimenas kai didaskalous G4166 G1320 (shepherds and teachers)
scripture.force: Christ gives five ministry gifts to the church. Some Pentecostal-charismatic and New Apostolic Reformation readings treat the apostolic and prophetic gifts as continuing, which would imply a non-tactile but still ongoing apostolic ministry. The classical Reformed-Baptist-Brethren reading treats apostles and prophets as foundational (per Eph 2:20) and ceased. The Catholic-Orthodox reading treats apostolic ministry as continuing through episcopal succession. The text itself does not lexically settle the continuing-or-foundational question; the verdict depends on hermeneutical framework. Complicates a single-reading verdict.

scripture.key_term: ἡμεῖς μάρτυρες hemeis martyres G3144 (we are witnesses); οὐ παντὶ τῷ λαῷ ἀλλὰ μάρτυσιν τοῖς προκεχειροτονημένοις ou panti to lao alla martysin tois prokecheirotonemenois G4401 (not to all the people but to witnesses chosen beforehand)
scripture.force: Peter names that the risen Christ appeared not to all the people but to witnesses chosen beforehand (prokecheirotonemenois G4401, perfect passive participle, having been chosen previously). The apostolic witness role is lexically tied to a closed pre-selected group of resurrection eyewitnesses. The text reinforces that the eyewitness aspect of apostleship is by canonical design non-perpetuating beyond the first-generation chosen witnesses.

scripture.key_term: θεμέλιον ἔθηκα themelion etheka G2310 G5087 (I laid a foundation); θεμέλιον γὰρ ἄλλον οὐδεὶς δύναται θεῖναι themelion gar allon oudeis dynatai theinai G2310 (no one can lay another foundation); Ἰησοῦς Χριστός Iesous Christos G2424 G5547
scripture.force: Paul names himself as a wise master-builder who laid the foundation (themelion etheka, aorist) and explicitly says no one can lay another foundation than Jesus Christ. Lexically the foundation is named once-laid, with Christ as the foundation. The text complicates a simple reading of Eph 2:20: there Christ is cornerstone with apostles and prophets as foundation, here Christ himself is the foundation laid by Paul's preaching. The metaphor flexes; the consistent lexical force is foundational-once-laid, not perpetuated through tactile succession.

scripture.key_term: τῇ ἅπαξ παραδοθείσῃ τοῖς ἁγίοις πίστει te hapax paradotheise tois hagiois pistei G530 G3860 G40 G4102 (the faith once for all delivered to the saints)
scripture.force: Jude names the faith as once for all (hapax G530) delivered (paradotheise G3860, aorist passive participle) to the saints. Lexically the faith-deposit is named as a closed, once-delivered tradition. The teaching-fidelity reading takes this as the substance of what continues from the apostles. The verb paradidomi G3860 is the same root as paratheke and parathou used in 2 Tim 2:2 of the teaching-deposit. Strong text for the teaching-deposit reading of apostolic continuity.

scripture.key_term: ἐξ ὑμῶν αὐτῶν ἀναστήσονται ἄνδρες λαλοῦντες διεστραμμένα ex hymon auton anastesontai andres lalountes diestrammena G1881 G1294 (from among yourselves men will arise speaking perverted things)
scripture.force: Paul warns the Ephesian elders that even from among the eldership itself men will arise teaching distorted things. The text complicates any reading that treats ordained-by-apostle laying-on-of-hands as a guarantee of doctrinal fidelity. Lexically Paul anticipates apostasy from within the line of named-and-commissioned elders. The teaching-fidelity-test of apostolic continuity is named as the diagnostic, not the line of commissioning.

competing_lens_verdicts.reasoning: Apostolic role is named as foundational eyewitness (Acts 1:22, 1 Cor 15:8, Acts 10:41) and as named foundation stones (Eph 2:20, Rev 21:14, limited to twelve). Succession that continues is teaching-deposit (2 Tim 2:2 parathou, Jude 3 hapax paradotheise). Hands-laying is commissioning practice but not the lexical mechanism of apostolic-office continuity. Paul's own apostleship explicitly bypasses tactile transmission (Gal 1:11-12).

Apostolic ministry continues through hands-laying transmission. 2 Tim 1:6 documents Paul laying hands on Timothy to transmit charisma. 1 Tim 4:14 documents the presbyterion's hands-laying. Acts 6:6, 13:3 document apostolic hands-laying for commissioning. Heb 6:2 names hands-laying as foundational doctrine. The patristic record from Clement of Rome ca 95 names a public succession of bishops appointed by the apostles, and Irenaeus ca 180 develops the argument explicitly. The foundational-eyewitness aspect ended, but the apostolic ministry of teaching authority and sacramental orders continues through the bishops.

Augsburg XXVIII names the ministry as commanded by Christ to preach the Gospel and administer the sacraments. Apostolic succession in the tactile-Catholic sense is not required, but the office of ministry by regular call and (where possible) episcopal ordination is preserved. The Lutheran position holds that proper ordination is desirable and traditional but not necessary by divine right. Both Catholic and Reformed readings have lexical footholds; the Augsburg position is a mediating one.

Some Pentecostal-charismatic, New Apostolic Reformation, and restorationist traditions read Eph 4:11 as naming a continuing apostolic gift apart from any tactile-succession requirement. They reject both Catholic tactile-succession and Reformed cessation of apostolic ministry. They affirm continuing apostolic ministry by direct charismatic appointment from Christ. This reading is itself contested and represents a minority position with significant lexical and historical questions.

hermeneutics.notes: All four readings operate within or adjacent to historic Christianity. The grammatico-historical reading alone does not settle the question because the New Testament names a closed foundational eyewitness role for the apostles, names a teaching-deposit that must continue, AND documents a practice of laying-on-of-hands commissioning. Each reading weights these data differently. The Protestant denial of tactile apostolic succession requires the hermeneutical judgment that hands-laying commissioning in the apostolic age did not constitute the transfer of apostolic office to perpetuating bishops. The Catholic affirmation requires the hermeneutical judgment that the New Testament hands-laying practice did constitute such transfer. Both judgments are within the bounds of orthodoxy. The Brethren-on-trial discipline applies: the Brethren denial is the user's inherited position and lacks unambiguous pan-canonical lexical mandate that would close the question against the historic Catholic-Orthodox reading.

counter_witness.anchor: Clement of Rome, 1 Clement chapters 42 and 44 (c. 95), ccel.org Schaff ANF Vol 1
counter_witness.key_phrase: The apostles received the gospel for us from the Lord Jesus Christ. Both these appointments were made in an orderly way. They appointed the first-fruits as bishops and deacons.

counter_witness.anchor: Ignatius of Antioch, Epistle to the Smyrnaeans chapter 8 (c. 110), ccel.org Schaff ANF Vol 1
counter_witness.key_phrase: Let no one do anything connected with the Church without the bishop. Let that be deemed a proper eucharist which is administered either by the bishop or by one to whom he has entrusted it.

counter_witness.anchor: Irenaeus, Against Heresies Book 3 chapter 3 (c. 180), ccel.org Schaff ANF Vol 1
counter_witness.key_phrase: The blessed apostles, then, having founded and built up the Church, committed into the hands of Linus the office of the episcopate.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church paragraphs 860-862, vatican.va
counter_witness.key_phrase: In the office of the apostles there is one aspect that cannot be transmitted: to be the chosen witnesses of the Lord's Resurrection. The bishops have by divine institution taken the place of the apostles.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession Article XXVIII Of Ecclesiastical Power, bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: The power of the Keys, or the power of the bishops, according to the Gospel, is a power or commandment of God, to preach the Gospel, to remit and retain sins, and to administer Sacraments.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article XXIII Of Ministering in the Congregation and Article XXXVI Of Consecration of Bishops, churchofengland.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: It is not lawful for any man to take upon him the office of public preaching, or ministering the Sacraments in the Congregation, before he be lawfully called, and sent to execute the same.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith Chapter 25 Of the Church and Chapter 30 Of Church Censures
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Lord Jesus, as king and head of His Church, has appointed a government in the hand of church-officers distinct from the civil magistrate. Church-officers are ordained by lawful presbyteries, not in tactile succession from the apostles.

counter_witness.anchor: Schleitheim Confession (1527), Article V Of Pastors in the Church of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: The pastor in the Church of God shall be one who is wholly according to the rule of Paul. The Anabaptist position rejects Catholic episcopal succession; pastors are chosen by the congregation under apostolic teaching.

counter_witness.anchor: United Methodist Articles of Religion, Methodist polity with itinerant bishops, umc.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: Methodist polity preserves bishops as superintendents of the connection. John Wesley ordained his own bishops outside Anglican apostolic succession when the Anglican bishops refused; succession is functional not tactile-canonical.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths and position on apostles, ag.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Assemblies of God rejects formal Catholic apostolic succession. Apostolic ministry continues in function through gifting and Word ministry, not through tactile transmission of episcopal orders.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, teaching on bishops and apostolic succession, oca.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Orthodox Church is governed by bishops who stand in unbroken apostolic succession. Each diocese is the local church gathered around its bishop in eucharistic communion with all other Orthodox bishops.

web.quote: themelio G2310 dat sg, foundation; apostolon G652 gen pl, apostles; akrogoniaiou G204, cornerstone. Foundation named in identity, once-laid metaphor; cornerstone is Christ himself.
ekousas G191 aor ind act 2sg you have heard; parathou G3908 aor impv mid 2sg entrust; pistois anthropois G4103 G444 faithful men; didaxai G1321 to teach. Teaching-deposit succession explicit.
martyra G3144 acc sg witness; anastaseos G386 gen sg of resurrection. Apostolic role requires direct eyewitness of resurrection, lexically non-perpetuating.
klerous G2819 lots; synkatepsephisthe G4785 was numbered with; hendeka apostolon G1733 G652. Matthias selected by lot not by laying on of hands from the eleven.
ektromati G1626 dat sg, untimely birth. Paul names himself as one untimely born, apostolic credential is having seen the risen Lord.
parelabon G3880 aor ind act 1sg I received; di apokalypseos G602 through revelation. Paul's commission explicitly bypasses human transmission chain.
themelious G2310 acc pl foundations; dodeka G1427 twelve; apostolon G652 of apostles. Twelve named foundation stones, closed and limited.
epitheseos ton cheiron mou G1936 G5495, through the laying on of my hands. Paul transmits charisma to Timothy by hands-laying. Catholic foundation text for tactile succession.
meta epitheseos ton cheiron tou presbyteriou G1936 G5495 G4244, with laying on of hands of the presbytery. Gift transmitted by collective hands-laying of elders.
apostolos G652 occurs 79-80 times in NT. Primarily the Twelve plus Paul; broader sense for Barnabas, possibly Andronicus and Junia; not extended to post-apostolic generations in NT itself.
epithesis G1936 (laying on) 4 NT occurrences plus Acts 13:3: Acts 8:18, 1 Tim 4:14, 2 Tim 1:6, Heb 6:2. Functions as commissioning and gift-imparting, not specified as transfer of episcopal orders.
CCC 860-862: In the office of the apostles one aspect cannot be transmitted: to be chosen witnesses of the Resurrection. Bishops by divine institution have taken the place of the apostles.

Irenaeus AH 3.3 c. 180: The blessed apostles, having founded and built up the Church, committed into the hands of Linus the office of the episcopate. Apostolic succession argued against gnostic claimants.
Augsburg XXVIII: power of the bishops is power to preach the Gospel and administer Sacraments. Lutheran position preserves episcopal function without requiring tactile apostolic succession.
Anglican Article XXIII: not lawful to preach or minister Sacraments unless lawfully called and sent. Article XXXVI defends the form of episcopal consecration as legitimate succession.
